summary: Stancil argues that the modern data stack’s fragmentation hides compounding costs: each new tool and higher-fidelity use case triggers cascading usage fees across vendors. Because data tooling is mostly services-oriented and priced inconsistently, the ecosystem is economically unstable unless it consolidates or builds better coordinating layers. The likely outcome is fewer dominant platforms with satellite products and stronger cost management.
money_quotes: In its normal operation, costs are intertwined and opaque.
Each category makes the data stack more technically robust—and more economically tenuous.
But the analogy is fatally flawed.
The demand for these tools is there, and more is coming.
To make this whole thing work, we don’t need less mass in the solar system; we just need more organization.
key_insight: The modern data stack’s economic problem isn’t demand but the fractured, opaque, usage-amplifying supply that makes costs balloon unless the market consolidates or coordinates.
